Title: Branched oligosaccharides cause atypical starch granule initiation in Arabidopsis chloroplasts

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: starch granule number/morphology via branched malto-oligosaccharides
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: transitory starch degradation linked to starch granule initiation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: endoamylolytic cleavage of starch surface glucans to release soluble malto-oligosaccharides, including branched species
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: genetic loss-of-function in amy3 isa3 and amy3 isa3 lda backgrounds; HPAEC-PAD of soluble malto-oligosaccharides; light microscopy; flow cytometry; SEM; starch quantification
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h light/12-h dark cycle; end of dark and end of light sampling
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: native mutant alleles; no transgene promoter used
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AMY3 promotes accumulation of branched malto-oligosaccharides that drive excess starch granule initiation; removing AMY3 eliminates branched malto-oligosaccharides and strongly reduces granule over-initiation while increasing starch excess
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AMY3 activity is required for accumulation of soluble branched malto-oligosaccharides in the isa3 lda background. 2. Loss of AMY3 in amy3 isa3 lda greatly reduces granule number per chloroplast and shifts starch toward fewer, much larger granules. 3. These data support AMY3-released branched oligosaccharides as primers for atypical granule initiation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Modulating AMY3 could alter starch granule initiation frequency and granule-size distribution, but may also exacerbate starch-excess phenotypes in degradation-defective backgrounds.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of AMY3 increases starch-excess phenotype in starch-breakdown mutants; chloroplast enlargement accompanies severe starch accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper treats AMY3 as a source of branched malto-oligosaccharide primers rather than a canonical granule-initiation protein.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: starch granule number/morphology via branched malto-oligosaccharides
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: transitory starch degradation linked to starch granule initiation
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: chloroplast starch debranching during starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: branched malto-oligosaccharides/limit dextrins from starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: hydrolyzes α-1,6-branch linkages in branched malto-oligosaccharides generated during chloroplast starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for preventing branched malto-oligosaccharide accumulation in combination with LDA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: single, double, and triple mutant analysis; HPAEC-PAD of branched malto-oligosaccharides; starch quantification; light microscopy; TEM; flow cytometry
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h light/12-h dark cycle; end of dark and end of light sampling
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: native mutant alleles; no transgene promoter used
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of ISA3 together with LDA causes accumulation of branched malto-oligosaccharides and a drastic increase in starch granule number per chloroplast; ISA3 single mutants mainly show starch excess with only slight granule-number increase
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ISA3 deficiency contributes to incomplete nighttime starch breakdown and starch excess. 2. Combined loss of ISA3 and LDA specifically causes accumulation of branched malto-oligosaccharides in chloroplasts. 3. The branched oligosaccharides in isa3 lda are associated with strong granule over-initiation, many small granules, and occasional ectopic granules near the envelope.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating ISA3 together with other debranching enzymes could change starch granule initiation and morphology, but severe starch breakdown defects make this strategy risky for crop performance.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Starch-excess phenotype, enlarged chloroplasts in higher-order mutants, altered granule morphology and chloroplast number.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ISA3 alone is not sufficient to generate the strong over-initiation phenotype; the causal phenotype requires combined loss with LDA and is influenced by AMY3.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: starch granule number/morphology via branched malto-oligosaccharides
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: transitory starch degradation linked to starch granule initiation
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: chloroplast starch debranching during starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: branched malto-oligosaccharides/limit dextrins from starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: hydrolyzes α-1,6-branch linkages in branched glucans during starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for preventing branched malto-oligosaccharide accumulation in combination with ISA3
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: single, double, and triple mutant analysis; HPAEC-PAD of branched malto-oligosaccharides; starch quantification; light microscopy; TEM; flow cytometry
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h light/12-h dark cycle; end of dark and end of light sampling
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: native mutant alleles; no transgene promoter used
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: LDA loss alone has little effect, but combined with ISA3 loss permits branched malto-oligosaccharide accumulation and strong starch granule over-initiation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LDA single mutants resemble wild type for branched malto-oligosaccharides and granule number. 2. In combination with ISA3 deficiency, LDA loss allows branched malto-oligosaccharides to accumulate. 3. This double-mutant state correlates with many small starch granules and atypical initiation sites.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: LDA manipulation may be useful only in combination strategies for tuning starch granule traits, but strong degradation defects and pleiotropic effects are major cautions.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In combination with isa3, causes starch excess and abnormal granule initiation/morphology.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functional effect is strongly context dependent; LDA is not a primary driver by itself in this study.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: canonical starch granule initiation and granule growth patterning
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: elongated α-1,4-glucan chains supporting granule initiation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: elongates malto-oligosaccharide primers using ADP-glucose during granule initiation and influences subsequent granule growth pattern
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for canonical granule initiation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: single and triple mutant analysis (ss4 and ss4 isa3 lda); HPAEC-PAD; light microscopy; flow cytometry; SBF-SEM; starch phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h light/12-h dark cycle; end of dark and end of light sampling
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: native mutant alleles; no transgene promoter used
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SS4 is essential for normal canonical granule initiation and granule shape, but branched-MOS-driven over-initiation in isa3 lda persists without SS4; loss of SS4 converts granules to rounder morphology and causes pale, small-rosette phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ss4 single mutants have sharply reduced granule number and large rounded granules. 2. In ss4 isa3 lda, high granule number persists, showing branched-MOS-driven over-initiation bypasses SS4-dependent canonical initiation. 3. SS4 still influences post-initiation granule growth, because small granules in ss4 isa3 lda retain the rounded ss4-like morphology.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SS4 is a strong lever for starch granule number and morphology, but altering it can impair growth and chloroplast function; useful for starch property engineering with caution.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Smaller rosette size, pale phenotype, many starch-free chloroplasts in ss4 single mutants.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study distinguishes SS4-dependent canonical initiation from SS4-independent atypical initiation driven by branched oligosaccharides.

Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: granule initiation factor / starch-binding regulatory protein
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PROTEIN TARGETING TO STARCH family; starch-binding protein
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: substrate/primer binding and targeting within canonical granule initiation machinery
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: supports canonical primer selection/binding for SS4-mediated granule initiation
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: acts with SS4; localization/function influenced by MFP1 according to discussion
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: essential for normal canonical starch granule initiation in Arabidopsis leaves, but not required for branched-MOS-driven atypical over-initiation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: single and triple mutant analysis (ptst2 and ptst2 isa3 lda); HPAEC-PAD; light microscopy; flow cytometry; starch phenotyping
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 12-h light/12-h dark cycle; end of dark and end of light sampling
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: native mutant alleles; no transgene promoter used
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PTST2 loss strongly reduces normal granule number in single mutants, but does not suppress granule over-initiation caused by branched malto-oligosaccharides in isa3 lda
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PTST2 is required for normal canonical starch granule initiation, as ptst2 chloroplasts contain few enlarged granules. 2. Branched malto-oligosaccharides still accumulate in ptst2 isa3 lda. 3. The high granule number and small-granule phenotype of isa3 lda largely persist without PTST2, indicating an independent initiation route.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PTST2 is useful to control starch granule number under the canonical pathway, but its manipulation may be overridden when alternative primer pools accumulate.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Single-mutant reduction in granule number with enlarged granules.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although not a transcription factor, PTST2 functions as a regulatory/initiation factor and is best placed in Regulation_Genes rather than Pathway_Genes in this study.
